title: What I’ve Learned After 2+ Years Testing AI Trading Bots
author: GordonLevinson
selftext:
I’ve been experimenting with AI trading bots since ChatGPT first came out a couple of years ago, and I’ve tested a lot of different setups since then.

Here’s what I’ve learned.

First — simply giving AI full authority to trade will not magically make you profitable.

A lot of people assume that more inputs = better decisions. That’s not always true. In fact, overloading a model with too many signals can reduce clarity and increase overtrading.

And overtrading is the silent killer.

Fees bleed slowly. Small unnecessary trades compound into negative expectancy.

What actually matters:

	•	Testing different prompts and structures

	•	Keeping inputs focused and intentional

	•	Stress-testing across different market regimes (chop vs trend, low vs high volatility, leverage vs spot)

	•	Measuring performance across time, not just short bursts

Only when a strategy survives multiple environments can you call it robust.

We’ve already seen public experiments (like Alpha Arena and others) where AI strategies achieved decent returns — for example, \~30% over a competition period. That proves one thing:

AI can generate edge under structured conditions.

But the real shift AI brings isn’t “easy money.”

It lowers the barrier.

Someone without a deep financial background can now experiment with structured strategies and iterate much faster than before.

The real question isn’t:

“Can AI make money trading?”

Under the right structure, yes.

The real question is:

Do you know how to structure and iterate your AI so it adapts to market conditions instead of overfitting to one regime?

Personally, what frustrated me most was iteration.

Every tiny adjustment meant editing code, redeploying, restarting processes, re-running backtests.

So I ended up building a platform to simplify that workflow — mainly to remove the constant infrastructure friction and focus on strategy logic instead. It’s more about experimentation and structured AI execution than “auto-profit.”

I’ve also been running an Arena-style environment (virtual capital, live market data, AI-only execution) to see how different structured strategies perform over time. The results are competitive, but more importantly, they’re realistic — including volatility and drawdowns.

Curious to hear from others here:

	•	Are you running AI bots live?

	•	What’s been your biggest challenge — model quality, structure, risk, or iteration?

	•	Do you think the edge is in the model itself or in how it’s deployed?

Happy to discuss.

comments.author: that_trading_guy
comments.body: I’m looking to buy a really good bot with solid data. Is yours for sale and on myfxbook?

replies.author: GordonLevinson
replies.body: Sorry I’m not selling my strategy but you can for sure test out you own strategies https://www.coreboundai.io/  just pick your AI to trade for you wisely, and do make sure to add struck risk controls

replies.author: Careless-Diamond-277
replies.body: Check Trade with pat he has a nice bot I bought it to and I am honestly happy with it I would say

comments.author: BerlinCode42
comments.body:
I think you do have a mistake i your approach using chatgpt for trading.
Those chatbot like chatgpt etc had their training phase and are fixed now. They dont learn new things. But the the task trading requires that price charts need to be scanned for unseen and subtile patterns. They need to learn those pattern before searching for them. 
Instead of chatgpt use neuronal networks that have been trained on price charts. Like "Historical Similarities" or "ANN Trend Prediction" on TradingView.
They realy use the power of AI for trading

replies.author: GordonLevinson
replies.body: I’m not really using an LLM as a chart-pattern detector or trying to make it learn hidden visual structures from scratch. The way I approach it is more as a structured decision layer on top of live market inputs — volatility, trend alignment, momentum context, fee impact, current position state. It’s less about teaching it new patterns and more about having it evaluate conditions consistently and act within strict risk rules. In trading, prediction accuracy alone rarely carries a system; regime filtering, risk control and execution discipline usually matter more. So I’m not replacing specialized time-series models — I’m focusing on building a decision framework that uses current information in a controlled way.

comments.author: The_Shadow_Tiger
comments.body:
Hi,

So on your platform we can build a trading bot and see how it performs using virtual capital, right?

And then if we see solid data and want to buid an EA for MT5 or a signals bot for TradingView, how your platform facilitate this?

Thanks in advance.

replies.author: GordonLevinson
replies.body:
Hi. Yes, you can deploy an AI trading agent using virtual capital to test its behavior under real market conditions.

If the performance is solid and you’re comfortable with the risk profile, you don’t need to export it to MT5 or TradingView. You can switch it directly to live trading mode within the same environment using the exact same AI agent and strategy you set.

replies.author: The_Shadow_Tiger
replies.body: That sounds amazing! Will need to test it out myself.

replies.author: GordonLevinson
replies.body: Will be worth having a look
